title: Rust, NPUs and a question I can't get out of my head
excerpt: Reflections on local inference hardware, NPUs, Rust, and the impact on business models if intelligence moves back to our own machines.
content: Over the past few years, most of the conversation around AI has been dominated by ever-larger models.
More parameters. More GPUs. More data centers. More compute concentrated in a handful of companies.
And for a long time I assumed that was the natural path of evolution: **AI lived in the cloud** and we simply consumed the results.
### The rise of local inference hardware
But I started noticing something interesting. More and more devices began incorporating **dedicated hardware** to accelerate local inference.
First phones, then notebooks. Now even consumer PCs appear with integrated NPUs as just another system component.
And that led me to think about something we might be taking for granted: **What if a significant part of AI stops running in the cloud?**
Because if we observe the direction of the industry, local inference has several advantages that are hard to ignore:
- Lower latency.
- Greater privacy.
- Lower operational cost.
- Offline operation.
And an experience that doesn't constantly depend on the quality of an internet connection.
### Why Rust at the edge?

In that context, **Rust** seems like a particularly interesting combination. Not because it's the trendy language, but because several of its characteristics seem to naturally fit this type of hardware:
- Memory safety without a garbage collector.
- Close-to-hardware control.
- High performance.
- Predictable resource consumption.
Characteristics that are especially attractive when working on devices with energy or thermal constraints.
### From the data center to hybrid architectures

The more I think about it, the more it reminds me of what happened at other moments in computing: for years we centralized resources, and then we found ways to distribute them again. Not as a complete replacement, but as a complement.
I don't imagine a world where large models disappear. There are tasks that will keep requiring enormous amounts of centralized compute. But I can imagine a **hybrid scenario**:
- Giant models trained in datacenters.
- Specialized models running locally.
- Simple decisions resolved on the device.
- Complex problems delegated to the cloud.
Something similar to how today we coexist with local and remote storage.
### The big question: what happens to the business model?
The interesting part is that this shift could have deeper implications than a simple technical improvement.
Because much of the current AI ecosystem is built on a continuous service model (SaaS). We pay a subscription, consume an API, depend on infrastructure maintained by third parties.

But if a device can locally run a significant portion of the capabilities we currently consume from the cloud, the value stops being exclusively in serving tokens from a datacenter. And starts shifting elsewhere: models, updates, data, experiences, or integrations.

**Perhaps the future won't be exclusively SaaS nor exclusively edge, but a combination of both.** A world where intelligence is distributed across devices and specialized services, and where the real challenge isn't just building larger models, but deciding where it makes sense to run them.
I don't have a clear answer yet. But every time I see local inference hardware advancing and the growth of Rust in high-performance systems, the question comes back:
**If AI really moves toward the edge, will the live service model as we know it today still make sense?**

title: RAG and a Reality That Usually Goes Unnoticed
excerpt: Over the last year, hundreds of tutorials appeared explaining how to build RAG systems. But the more I work with these architectures, the more I am convinced we are focusing on the wrong place.
content: Over the last year, hundreds of tutorials appeared explaining how to build RAG systems.
The recipe is usually quite similar:
- Documents are loaded.
- Embeddings are generated.
- They are stored in a vector database.
- An LLM is connected.
And that's it: we have a chatbot over our data.
But the more I work with this type of architecture, the more I am convinced that we are focusing on the wrong place.
Because when a RAG system fails, the usual reaction is to blame the model.
"The LLM hallucinated."
"The model gave a poor answer."
"We need a more powerful one."
However, many times the problem appears long before the model starts generating text.
### The question that really matters
When a user makes a query, the LLM does not see the entire document database.
It only sees the context that someone decided to hand over to it.
And that "someone" is the retriever.
In other words:
The model can only reason about the information it receives.
If the context is incorrect, incomplete, or irrelevant, even the best LLM in the world will struggle to produce a good response.
That's why I started thinking that, in many RAG projects, the most important component is not the model.
It's the retrieval system.
### The retriever as the true bottleneck
It is tempting to spend weeks comparing models.
- GPT.
- Claude.
- Gemini.
- Llama.
- Mistral.
But in many cases the final performance is dominated by a much simpler question:
Are we retrieving the correct documents?
Because an LLM can summarize, infer, or rephrase.
What it cannot do is invent information it never received.
And there lies an uncomfortable reality:
Two systems can use the exact same model and get radically different results simply because they retrieve different contexts.
### The importance of measuring retrieval
Curiously, many RAG implementations are evaluated solely by looking at the final response.
But that mixes two distinct problems:
- Retrieval quality.
- Generation quality.
If a response is incorrect, was the relevant document not retrieved?
Or was it retrieved but the model interpreted it incorrectly?
Without specific metrics, it is impossible to know.
That's why metrics like **Recall@K**, **Precision@K**, or **MRR** are usually more important than they seem.
Not because they are academic metrics.
But because they allow us to answer a fundamental question:
Was the correct information available to the model?
Before evaluating the LLM, it is wise to evaluate the retriever.
### Embeddings: useful, but not magical
Another interesting phenomenon is how embeddings became almost synonymous with RAG.
It seems that any search problem must be solved using semantic similarity.
And they certainly have huge advantages.
They allow finding related concepts even when the user does not use the exact same words that appear in the document.
But they also have limitations.
There are queries where lexical matching is still extremely valuable.
- Product names.
- Codes.
- Versions.
- Identifiers.
- Invoice numbers.
- Regulatory references.
In these cases, classic algorithms like **BM25** can easily outperform a purely vector search.
And that is precisely why modern systems usually combine both approaches.
Not because embeddings are insufficient.
But because each method captures different signals.
### Hybrid retrieval
The more cases I see, the harder it is for me to think of BM25 as an ancient technology.
In fact, many modern architectures end up using:
- Lexical search.
- Vector search.
- Subsequent re-ranking.
All working together.
Because the goal is not to find "similar" documents.
The goal is to find the correct documents.
And both strategies provide complementary information.
### What the LLM actually does
Another idea that I find interesting is that we often attribute capabilities to the model that actually depend on retrieval.
An LLM rarely "knows" anything within a RAG system.
What it does is consume context, synthesize information, and build a coherent response.
Therefore, increasing the model size does not always solve quality problems.
If the context is bad, a more powerful model might produce a more elegant explanation.
But it will still be starting from incorrect information.
### The safe nets that are often forgotten
Something that also appears frequently is the absence of safety mechanisms.
Many implementations assume that there will always be a valid response within the document database.
But that is not necessarily true.
What happens when no relevant document passes a minimum threshold?
What happens when retrieval returns contradictory information?
What happens when confidence is too low?
In some scenarios, the best possible answer is not to answer.
It is to acknowledge that there is not enough evidence.
And paradoxically, that usually builds more trust than an apparently convincing answer.
### A particularly dangerous mistake
There is another pattern that appears time and again.
We train or fine-tune a model using the exact same information we then retrieve via RAG.
At first glance, it seems reasonable.
But many times we end up duplicating knowledge.
The model already contains the information.
The retriever delivers it again.
And then it becomes difficult to understand which component is adding value.
Worse yet: when documents change, the knowledge stored during training starts to diverge from the retrieved knowledge.
And inconsistencies that are hard to explain appear.
That's why I see more and more value in separating responsibilities.
- The model to reason.
- The document base to provide up-to-date knowledge.
- And the retriever to connect both worlds.
### The question that keeps spinning in my head
For a long time, I thought that the evolution of RAG systems would be dominated by increasingly capable models.
But the more I observe real-world implementations, the more it seems to me that the difference between a mediocre experience and an excellent one usually appears much earlier.
In retrieval.
In evaluation.
In the quality of the context.
In the decisions that determine which information reaches the model and which does not.
And that leaves me with a question that appears with increasing frequency:
If the LLM can only reason about what it receives, are we still underestimating the role of the retriever in modern AI systems?

title: Are We Measuring the Right Performance of Our Models?
excerpt: Are traditional metrics reliable? Reflections on stability, generalization, random seed effects, and the limits of theoretical bounds in machine learning.
content: When we evaluate a machine learning model, we usually look at familiar metrics: accuracy, AUC, F1-score, RMSE, or some similar variant. They are useful metrics because they answer a concrete question: how well does the model predict on a given dataset?
However, I find myself wondering more and more if we are leaving out another dimension that is just as important: the stability of the solution found.

In neural networks, there is a long-standing discussion about the geometry of the optimization process. People talk about valleys, plateaus, local minima, or "flat" regions of the loss landscape. There are hypotheses—still debated—suggesting that flatter solutions might generalize better, although this is not completely settled.
But the question that interests me is not just whether this is true in neural networks, but whether an equivalent idea exists in models that do not have that type of continuous geometry.

In methods like boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost), the model does not explore a loss landscape in the same sense. But there is a sequential construction process where local decisions—which split to make, which variables to use, how to subsample—determine the final model.
And in that process, something appears that is sometimes underestimated: the dependency of the result on small perturbations in training.
The "same score, different model" problem
Imagine two models with the same AUC of 0.85.
The first is very consistent: it changes little when retrained with different seeds or small variations of the dataset.
The second has high variability: small perturbations generate different models, with different decisions.
Traditional metrics consider them equivalent.
But intuitively, they are not.
One seems to capture a more "stable" structure of the problem. The other seems more dependent on the randomness of the training process.
This is not just intuition
This concern is appearing more and more in recent literature.

For example, Schader et al. (2024) show that in modern machine learning models applied to causal inference—including doubly robust estimators like AIPTW or TMLE—the choice of random seed can significantly change the final results, even altering scientific conclusions in some cases. [Don’t let your analysis go to seed](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12166761/)

In their work, they show that different seeds can produce confidence intervals that not only vary in magnitude, but can lead to opposite interpretations of the causal effect. They also propose stabilization strategies, such as averaging over multiple seeds, to reduce this variability.
The direct implication: even when the model "works well" on average, its result can be sensitive to arbitrary details of the training process.
Beyond the empirical phenomenon: what does "estimability" mean?
This type of variability is not just an implementation problem. There is a deeper question:
What does it mean for a model to be "stable" or "estimable" in the first place?
From learning theory, this connects to the idea that not all algorithms admit good generalization guarantees in the same sense.

Gastpar et al. (2024) study exactly this point from a more fundamental perspective. In [Which Algorithms Have Tight Generalization Bounds?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.01969), they analyze when it is possible to build genuinely informative (tight) generalization bounds, and when those guarantees necessarily become weak or empty in certain regimes—particularly in overparameterized contexts.

A key idea of the work is that it is not enough to look at the hypothesis class or the dataset: the interaction between algorithm, distribution, and the stability of the procedure is central to understanding whether generalization can be controlled in a non-trivial way.
In this sense, stability is not just a practical property: it appears as a structural condition behind when we can or cannot trust certain types of guarantees.
Two perspectives on the same problem
Seen together, these two works point to complementary things:
Schader et al.: show that stability matters empirically, even in modern causal inference pipelines.
Gastpar et al.: suggest that stability may be part of the fundamental limits of what we can theoretically guarantee.
One looks at what happens in practice. The other looks at what is possible to promise in principle.
So, what are we really measuring?
When we say a model "has good performance," we are measuring something important but incomplete.
We are measuring the average result.
But we are not necessarily measuring:
how sensitive that result is to training noise,
how dependent it is on arbitrary decisions like the seed,
or how "stable" the solution is within the space of possible equivalent models.
And perhaps that matters just as much as the score itself.
Closing
Maybe the problem isn't finding the model with the best performance.
Maybe the problem is understanding whether the performance we see is a robust property of the phenomenon... or just a particular realization of a more unstable process.
And if two models have the same AUC, perhaps the right question isn't which one is better.
But which one is more reliable when the training changes slightly.

title: My Journey Into Quantitative Finance
excerpt: Reflections on transitioning from traditional Data Science to the fascinating world of complex systems under uncertainty and algorithmic trading.
content: For a long time, I viewed Data Science through the lens of predictive models, dashboards, and business metrics. I was also very drawn to the world of computer vision + augmented reality.
But due to how unviable it was—at least for me—and the niche, hyper-specialized market that the adoption of these technologies entails, it didn't end up convincing me.
### Initial Skepticism and Social Media Noise
At some point, I started getting interested in the **Quant world**, a place where there is constant uncertainty and systems changing all the time.
And curiously, what attracted me the most was not the idea of 'making money.'

Especially because I always had a rather negative connotation of the subject. I would look from the corner of my eye at the thousands of people on social media promoting videos and courses on 'get rich in X days,' each one more specialized and successful than the last.

The classic secret method that 'they don't want you to know.' Most of them showing themselves surrounded by cars and luxury lifestyles. A fantasy too obvious to buy into. Rather than attracting me, it repelled me.
### A More Serious Approach: Meeting Reality
Until I realized that a completely different level existed, one that was completely devoid of that aesthetic.

One day I came across analyses by [@JoseLuisCavatv](https://www.youtube.com/@JoseLuisCavatv) and it seemed like a much more serious and interesting perspective. I started listening to his videos in the background every day and, almost without realizing it, it became a routine.
He talked about what was happening in the world and how the markets might react.

Little by little I understood more things: the behavior of commodities, geopolitics, the influence of laws, business meetings, or major economic decisions. And the more I understood, the more interested I became.

In the middle of this, I discovered that Yahoo wasn't just a website for emails, and I found its historical reconstructed data section. I ended up building my own datasets to play around, explore, and experiment.
At the same time, I also started to 'train' my YouTube and Instagram algorithm, prioritizing more financial, technical, and IT-hybrid content.
### The Language of Complex Systems
And that's when something I didn't expect to find so entertaining appeared: **the language**.
Suddenly terms like *alpha, beta, drawdown, slippage, hedging* or *market microstructure* popped up.
At first they seemed like another language, but precisely that need to understand what they meant was one of the things that kept me diving deeper.
Because behind each term was a **mix of math, psychology, risk, and engineering**.
And it was then that I started to understand why financial markets are one of the few environments where probability theory, human behavior, and distributed systems constantly clash.
### Decisions Under Uncertainty
As a result of this journey, almost without realizing it, I started thinking differently.
The Fed meetings, the chaotic 'good afternoon' of Jerome Powell, and all the subsequent movement it generated started to seem genuinely fun to observe.
Because behind that was something bigger: millions of people reacting, systems adjusting in real-time, and markets constantly reinterpreting expectations.
From a more personal standpoint, I also started to focus much more on **financial responsibility**.

Trying to take the knowledge and interests I was gathering, adapt them, and apply them to my own life. And that's when I started breaking some ideas that I previously saw as normal, like the phrase: *'a dollar more or a dollar less doesn't make you richer or poorer'*. It's a linear view.
Because in the end, we all have human biases. We tend to underestimate exponential growth, misinterpret percentages, or minimize small decisions repeated over time.
And that made me change the way I view finance. Not just as money, but as **complex systems** where small decisions, risk management, and human behavior can completely alter an outcome.
Something akin to when Gohan in DBZ discovers that the reality he saw was much bigger than he imagined.
### Robustness Over Perfection
And that's when I started to notice a huge difference compared to many traditional Machine Learning projects.

In most of ML, the goal is usually to optimize precision on relatively stable data. But in algorithmic trading, **the environment changes constantly**. Patterns disappear, the market evolves, and a strategy that worked yesterday might stop working tomorrow.
I found that fascinating because it forces you to think differently.
It's not enough to just build an 'intelligent' model. You also have to think about robustness, adaptability, risk management, and the behavior of the entire system.
**Robustness matters more than perfection. Risk matters more than being right.** And understanding how people, markets, and algorithms react matters as much as the mathematical model itself.

Over time, I realized that quantitative finance lives exactly at the point where several things that have always interested me converge: programming, data science, and complex systems under uncertainty.
__IMAGE__
I'm still in the early stages of the journey, but I'm more convinced than ever that this is the kind of problems I want to solve.
